facts.claim: DNS resolution typically takes between 10–100ms depending on the resolver and network conditions, with <50ms being preferred for good user experience
TLS handshake with TLS 1.2 adds 30-50 milliseconds of latency for initial connections; TLS 1.3 saves 1 RTT vs TLS 1.2 due to fewer roundtrips
TLS session resumption reduces handshake overhead to under 5 milliseconds on subsequent queries by avoiding key exchange
TCP three-way handshake takes approximately 1.5 × RTT (typically 75 to 300 milliseconds) before application data can be transferred
HTTP/2 with TLS 1.2 requires at least 4 network round trips before first HTTP response; HTTP/2 with TLS 1.3 requires 3 RTT; HTTP/3 with QUIC requires 1-RTT for connection establishment
HTTP/3 can establish secure connections up to 50% faster than HTTP/2 by combining TLS handshake with connection setup in a single step
OCSP stapling can improve connection times by up to 30% by caching certificate status on the server and delivering it during TLS handshake
Cloudflare DNS consistently achieves 5–10ms latency in North America, Europe, and Asia, generally 2-5ms faster on average than Google Public DNS globally

Layer 4 load balancers make routing decisions based on IPs and TCP/UDP ports without decrypting content; Layer 7 load balancers terminate connections and route based on HTTP headers, URLs, and cookie data
Good TTFB values are 0.8 seconds or less; typical good TTFB is 200 milliseconds or less; poor TTFB is greater than 1.8 seconds
Static resources should use Cache-Control: max-age with long TTL (6+ months) and immutable directive; version/hash in URL prevents stale caching
Dynamic HTML and API responses should use Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache to ensure freshness; no-cache allows conditional requests
TCP slow start begins with initial window size (IW) of 2-10 MSS depending on maximum segment size; window effectively doubles each RTT during exponential growth phase
ALPN (Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation) completes protocol negotiation as part of TLS handshake without additional roundtrips; h3 ALPN ID indicates HTTP/3 over QUIC v1
Around 40% of HTTPS connections are resumptions; with 0-RTT, a round trip can be eliminated for most resumed sessions
Session cache can fall under memory pressure during traffic spikes, causing even 'fast' resumed sessions to queue behind full handshakes, increasing latency from 20-30ms to 5 seconds
Connection pooling with keep-alive can save 50-100ms of latency per new TLS connection; at 1000 requests/second, this overhead alone would saturate a database without pooling

Chrome DevTools categorizes request delays as Queueing (waiting for available TCP connection or higher-priority requests), Stalled (after connection start), and Waiting (TTFB, time for server to prepare response)
Wix's internal testing showed HTTP/3 delivers 33% faster connection setup and 20% better LCP at 75th percentile, often improving LCP by over 500ms
Network waterfall analysis requires examining DNS lookup, TCP connection, TLS negotiation, request send, wait for first byte (TTFB), and response receive in sequence

examples.scenario: Netflix edge load balancing during red-black deployments and auto-scaling events
examples.problem: Cold servers post-startup caused slow connection establishment; session resumption cache under memory pressure prevented optimization from working

examples.scenario: Incomplete TLS certificate chains in mobile environments
examples.problem: Desktop browser testing passed because Chrome and Firefox cache intermediate certificates; mobile browsers and API clients failed silently in production with missing intermediates
Serving content from distant servers added 200-500ms to TTFB; CDN edge servers reduced latency by serving from points of presence closer to users

examples.scenario: Java 21 Virtual Threads causing socket stalls
examples.problem: Netflix services on Java 21 with SpringBoot 3 and embedded Tomcat experienced intermittent timeouts and sockets stuck in closeWait state, degrading connection responsiveness
Browser limits to 6 TCP connections per origin in HTTP/1.1; HTTP/2 opens single connection multiplexed, reducing connection overhead by 83% when all 6 connections would have been needed

analogies.analogy: Like a phone call handshake: you dial (SYN), they pick up and acknowledge (SYN-ACK), you confirm they're there (ACK) — only then can you speak. Until all three steps complete, no conversation happens.

Like recognizing a repeat customer at a store: the first visit requires full handshake (ID check, account setup, etc.); subsequent visits with the same customer skip the setup and go straight to service. A session ID or ticket is like a frequent-customer card.

Like looking up a friend's address in a phonebook before driving to their house: if the lookup takes 50ms and the drive takes 100ms, you've wasted 33% of your journey time before leaving. Caching the address (DNS caching) eliminates this waste.
Like carrying a letter from the bank certifying your identity rather than having the merchant call the bank every time: eliminates the roundtrip to verify without losing security.

Layer 4 is like a postal service sorting mail by zip code (IP/port) without reading envelopes; Layer 7 is like a smart receptionist who reads the letter, understands the request, and routes it to the right department based on content.
Like a returning customer who can immediately order from memory without repeating their address and payment info: the handshake happens in parallel with the first request, not before it.
Like gradually increasing water flow through a pipe: start with 2-4 segments, double the flow with each ACK received. This probes the network's capacity without flooding it immediately.
Like maintaining standing tables in a restaurant instead of building and tearing down tables for each customer: the overhead of setup (TLS 50-100ms per connection) is amortized across many requests.

key_numbers.context: Fresh DNS resolution depends on resolver proximity and network conditions
Session resumption with TLS 1.3; skips key exchange
Connection establishment overhead before any data transfer
TCP handshake (1) + TLS key exchange (2) + HTTP request (1)
Combines TLS and connection setup; ALPN negotiation included

key_numbers.value: Up to 30% reduction in connection time
key_numbers.context: Eliminates roundtrip to CA's OCSP responder
Average 2-5ms faster than Google Public DNS globally

key_numbers.value: 0.8 seconds or less; optimal is 200ms or less
2-10 MSS depending on maximum segment size

key_numbers.metric: TLS handshake latency under traffic spike
key_numbers.context: Session cache eviction under high load causes full handshakes to queue
Reusing persistent connections eliminates handshake overhead

key_numbers.value: 33% faster setup, 20% better LCP at P75 (Wix testing)
key_numbers.context: Often improves LCP by >500ms in real-world conditions
Varies by application (VoIP <100ms, gaming <50ms, web APIs <100ms ideal)
Browser limit; HTTP/2 multiplexes single connection

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTPS is always slow due to TLS handshake overhead
common_misconceptions.reality: TLS 1.3 + session resumption reduces overhead to <5ms on repeat connections; initial overhead is mostly recovered by modern optimizations like 0-RTT and OCSP stapling

common_misconceptions.misconception: You must include the root certificate in the TLS certificate chain
common_misconceptions.reality: Root certificates are already in client trust stores; including them wastes bandwidth and slows connections. Only serve the server cert + intermediates.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Desktop browser testing is sufficient for HTTPS certificate validation
common_misconceptions.reality: Desktop browsers cache intermediate certificates locally, masking incomplete chains. Mobile browsers and API clients will fail silently in production without proper intermediates.

common_misconceptions.misconception: DNS is a negligible part of connection latency
common_misconceptions.reality: DNS adds 10-100ms per fresh lookup; a page with 20 domains means 20 DNS queries. This compounds significantly for first visits without caching.
Without pooling, 50-100ms TLS overhead per connection multiplied across requests becomes a critical bottleneck (e.g., 1000 req/s = 50-100 seconds of overhead per second of traffic)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Layer 7 load balancing is always better than Layer 4
common_misconceptions.reality: Layer 4 is faster and simpler for stateless routing; Layer 7 adds latency by terminating connections and inspecting content. Use Layer 4 when content-based routing isn't needed.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Session resumption always works under high load
common_misconceptions.reality: Session cache can be evicted under memory pressure during traffic spikes, forcing servers to do full handshakes and queuing connections, increasing latency from 20-30ms to 5+ seconds

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP/3 is necessary for all applications
common_misconceptions.reality: HTTP/3 benefits are most pronounced for high-latency networks and initial connections. For LAN or persistent connections, the 33% speedup may be negligible relative to application logic latency.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TTFB and LCP are the same metric
common_misconceptions.reality: TTFB is only the time to first byte; LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) includes rendering and is what users perceive as 'page load speed'

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cache-Control max-age should be short to minimize staleness
common_misconceptions.reality: Static assets (CSS, JS, images) benefit from 6-month max-age + URL versioning; short TTLs increase bandwidth and CPU. Staleness risk is version-based, not TTL-based.
